oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.

• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.

dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.

If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.

Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.

• Evidence:

Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)



TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22

Overall Severity: LOW

2026-06-29T00:00
❌ failure4.224.22-e2e-telco5g-ptp256mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
2 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22

TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.

Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node (cnfdf32) had no GNSS lock throughout the run, blocking all 5 WPC GM tests.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GNSS signal unavailable on WPC T-GM node (affects all 5 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

gnss[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 gnss_status -1 offset -1 s0

GM[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 T-GM-STATUS s0

event.go:528] dpll State -2, gnss State s0, tsphc state s0, gm state s0

```

(repeats every second from 02:37:18Z through 03:07:42Z — the entire TGM test window)


First failing test: could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-lnbks container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded


All subsequent: [FAILED] Timed out after 300.000s. Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM — Expected : false to be true (ptp.go:3457)


• Root Cause: Node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com (pod linuxptp-daemon-lnbks / linuxptp-daemon-p7c88) reported gnss_status -1 (no valid satellite fix) and dpll State -2 (FREERUN/uninitialized) from the very first second of the TGM test suite. With GNSS perpetually in FREERUN (s0), ts2phc cannot discipline the clock and ptp4l never reaches clock class 6 (LOCKED). The wait-for-clock-class helper at ptp.go:3457 exhausts its 300–340 s timeout on every WPC GM test. The DPLL later shows DPLL_LOCKED_HO_ACQ (s2) — holdover from a prior run — but the GNSS receiver itself never provides a valid signal during this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change involved; this is a hardware/infrastructure condition.
• Is it a flake? Yes — GNSS signal availability on bare-metal lab nodes is environment-dependent and can be intermittently lost without any code change.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate the GNSS antenna/receiver on cnfdf32 (check antenna cable, sky view obstruction, or u-blox receiver firmware reset). Retry the job; if GNSS is restored, all 5 tests should pass.
• Affected tests:
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on logs [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on metrics [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM ts2phc termination tests Continuously terminating ts2phc triggers FREERUN event and then recovers to LOCKED [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — all 5 tests share the exact same upstream cause: GNSS receiver on cnfdf32 stuck in FREERUN (s0) with gnss_status -1. No common software regression; purely hardware-level.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.23 — all 5 failures are in the [tgm-serial] suite on cnfdf32; bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc suites all passed (142 pass, 127 skip, 5 error total).


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.23 (running on cnfdf32 as linuxptp-daemon-lnbks/linuxptp-daemon-p7c88)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node (cnfdf32) had no GNSS lock throughout the run, blocking all 5 WPC GM tests.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GNSS signal unavailable on WPC T-GM node (affects all 5 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

gnss[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 gnss_status -1 offset -1 s0

GM[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 T-GM-STATUS s0

event.go:528] dpll State -2, gnss State s0, tsphc state s0, gm state s0

```

(repeats every second from 02:37:18Z through 03:07:42Z — the entire TGM test window)


First failing test: could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-lnbks container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded


All subsequent: [FAILED] Timed out after 300.000s. Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM — Expected : false to be true (ptp.go:3457)

With GNSS perpetually in FREERUN (s0), ts2phc cannot discipline the clock and ptp4l never reaches clock class 6 (LOCKED). The wait-for-clock-class helper at ptp.go:3457 exhausts its 300–340 s timeout on every WPC GM test. The DPLL later shows DPLL_LOCKED_HO_ACQ (s2) — holdover from a prior run — but the GNSS receiver itself never provides a valid signal during this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change involved; this is a hardware/infrastructure condition.
• Is it a flake? Yes — GNSS signal availability on bare-metal lab nodes is environment-dependent and can be intermittently lost without any code change.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate the GNSS antenna/receiver on cnfdf32 (check antenna cable, sky view obstruction, or u-blox receiver firmware reset). Retry the job; if GNSS is restored, all 5 tests should pass.
• Affected tests:
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on logs [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on metrics [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM ts2phc termination tests Continuously terminating ts2phc triggers FREERUN event and then recovers to LOCKED [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — all 5 tests share the exact same upstream cause: GNSS receiver on cnfdf32 stuck in FREERUN (s0) with gnss_status -1. No common software regression; purely hardware-level.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.23 — all 5 failures are in the [tgm-serial] suite on cnfdf32; bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc suites all passed (142 pass, 127 skip, 5 error total).


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.23 (running on cnfdf32 as linuxptp-daemon-lnbks/linuxptp-daemon-p7c88)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node (cnfdf32) had no GNSS lock throughout the run, blocking all 5 WPC GM tests.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GNSS signal unavailable on WPC T-GM node (affects all 5 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

gnss[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 gnss_status -1 offset -1 s0

GM[1782614238]:[ts2phc.0.config] ens7f0 T-GM-STATUS s0

event.go:528] dpll State -2, gnss State s0, tsphc state s0, gm state s0

```

(repeats every second from 02:37:18Z through 03:07:42Z — the entire TGM test window)


First failing test: could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-lnbks container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded


All subsequent: [FAILED] Timed out after 300.000s. Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM — Expected : false to be true (ptp.go:3457)


• Root Cause: Node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com (pod linuxptp-daemon-lnbks / linuxptp-daemon-p7c88) reported gnss_status -1 (no valid satellite fix) and dpll State -2 (FREERUN/uninitialized) from the very first second of the TGM test suite. With GNSS perpetually in FREERUN (s0), ts2phc cannot discipline the clock and ptp4l never reaches clock class 6 (LOCKED). The wait-for-clock-class helper at ptp.go:3457 exhausts its 300–340 s timeout on every WPC GM test. The DPLL later shows DPLL_LOCKED_HO_ACQ (s2) — holdover from a prior run — but the GNSS receiver itself never provides a valid signal during this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change involved; this is a hardware/infrastructure condition.
• Is it a flake? Yes — GNSS signal availability on bare-metal lab nodes is environment-dependent and can be intermittently lost without any code change.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate the GNSS antenna/receiver on cnfdf32 (check antenna cable, sky view obstruction, or u-blox receiver firmware reset). Retry the job; if GNSS is restored, all 5 tests should pass.
• Affected tests:
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on logs [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Verification Tests is verifying WPC GM state based on metrics [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM ts2phc termination tests Continuously terminating ts2phc triggers FREERUN event and then recovers to LOCKED [Serial]
• [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — all 5 tests share the exact same upstream cause: GNSS receiver on cnfdf32 stuck in FREERUN (s0) with gnss_status -1. No common software regression; purely hardware-level.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.23 — all 5 failures are in the [tgm-serial] suite on cnfdf32; bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc suites all passed (142 pass, 127 skip, 5 error total).


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.23 (running on cnfdf32 as linuxptp-daemon-lnbks/linuxptp-daemon-p7c88)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: LOW

2026-06-28T00:01
❌ failure4.234.23-e2e-telco5g-ptp-upstream148mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
2 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW

2026-06-28T05:00
⚠️ Test Failures
❌ failure5.05.0-e2e-telco5g-ptp354mtest_flake` (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + `test_regression` (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync `0459bc0`) CRITICAL
WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.
25 failed tests
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.




Per-Suite Details



dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualfollower ClockSync tests timed out waiting for node cnfdf31's follower interfaces to reach LOCKED state because the openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 was never emitted by linuxptp-daemon, and ens2f3np3 was stuck in LISTENING rather than SLAVE role.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Slave node never locked — missing clock_state metric and port stuck in LISTENING (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

Tests 1 & 2 — identical error

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig test-slave1

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="ptp/clock-under-test",

slaveIfs=[ens2f0np0 ens2f3np3]). Per-interface results:

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found,

available op[tions: ...]

```

```

Test 3 — additional symptom

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig temp

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="",

slaveIfs=[ens2f3np3 ens2f0np0]). Per-interface results:

ens2f3np3: role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING)

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found

```

```

Broader context from other suites (junit-failures.txt)

parentPortIdentity.clockIdentity not found in pmc output for /var/run/ptp4l.1.config:

bad command: GET

bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET

```

```

tgm suite (junit-failures.txt)

Expected ptp4l clock class state to eventually be Locked (class 6)

...clock class: 248 (← default/FREERUN value, never reached 6)

```

• Root Cause: The openshift_ptp_clock_state metric (registered in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/metrics/register.go:57-63 with labels {process, node, iface}) is only set when UpdateClockStateMetrics() in pkg/metrics/exporter.go:15-23 is called with a servo state change for that interface. A metric label combination {node=cnfdf31, iface=ens2f0np0} being entirely absent means linuxptp-daemon on cnfdf31 never processed a servo state log line for ens2f0np0 — i.e., ptp4l never elected that port as SLAVE and never emitted a servo lock event. The explicit role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING) in Test 3 for ens2f3np3 corroborates this: both follower ports are stuck in LISTENING/unelected state. The tgm suite failures (clock class 248 = freerun, never 6) indicate the upstream Grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock; with the T-GM in freerun the entire downstream chain — BC → dualfollower slave — cannot lock, which is consistent with all suites failing in the same run. Additionally, the pmc bad command: GET / PARENT_DATA_SET errors in the dualnicbc suite (same run, linuxptp-daemon branch release-5.0) indicate a ptp4l management interface issue. The commit OCPBUGS-90708: Sync from upstream (22-Jun-2026) in linuxptp-daemon (merged 2026-06-23, two days after this run) and the commit OCPBUGS-88369: Fix CLOCK_REALTIME dual-publisher conflict in ParseTBCLogs in cloud-event-proxy (merged 2026-06-25) were both pending at the time of this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Likely pre-dates the fix in linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (OCPBUGS-90708 / CNF-24996, merged 2026-06-23, SHA 0459bc0) and cloud-event-proxy PR #701 (OCPBUGS-88369, merged 2026-06-25, SHA fc5d567) — both merged after this CI run on 2026-06-21. The breaking change introducing the regression is Unknown (upstream/downstream commit not visible in the shallow clone).
• Is it a flake? No — all four test suites in the same job (dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, tgm) failed with consistent, correlated errors. The T-GM staying at clock class 248 is a systematic fa


TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.




Per-Suite Details



dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)

• Group 1: Slave node never locked — missing clock_state metric and port stuck in LISTENING (affects: all 3 tests)

The explicit role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING) in Test 3 for ens2f3np3 corroborates this: both follower ports are stuck in LISTENING/unelected state. The tgm suite failures (clock class 248 = freerun, never 6) indicate the upstream Grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock; with the T-GM in freerun the entire downstream chain — BC → dualfollower slave — cannot lock, which is consistent with all suites failing in the same run. The breaking change introducing the regression is Unknown (upstream/downstream commit not visible in the shallow clone).

TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.




Per-Suite Details



dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualfollower ClockSync tests timed out waiting for node cnfdf31's follower interfaces to reach LOCKED state because the openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 was never emitted by linuxptp-daemon, and ens2f3np3 was stuck in LISTENING rather than SLAVE role.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Slave node never locked — missing clock_state metric and port stuck in LISTENING (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

Tests 1 & 2 — identical error

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig test-slave1

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="ptp/clock-under-test",

slaveIfs=[ens2f0np0 ens2f3np3]). Per-interface results:

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found,

available op[tions: ...]

```

```

Test 3 — additional symptom

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig temp

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="",

slaveIfs=[ens2f3np3 ens2f0np0]). Per-interface results:

ens2f3np3: role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING)

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found

```

```

Broader context from other suites (junit-failures.txt)

parentPortIdentity.clockIdentity not found in pmc output for /var/run/ptp4l.1.config:

bad command: GET

bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET

```

```

tgm suite (junit-failures.txt)

Expected ptp4l clock class state to eventually be Locked (class 6)

...clock class: 248 (← default/FREERUN value, never reached 6)

```

• Root Cause: The openshift_ptp_clock_state metric (registered in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/metrics/register.go:57-63 with labels {process, node, iface}) is only set when UpdateClockStateMetrics() in pkg/metrics/exporter.go:15-23 is called with a servo state change for that interface. A metric label combination {node=cnfdf31, iface=ens2f0np0} being entirely absent means linuxptp-daemon on cnfdf31 never processed a servo state log line for ens2f0np0 — i.e., ptp4l never elected that port as SLAVE and never emitted a servo lock event. The explicit role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING) in Test 3 for ens2f3np3 corroborates this: both follower ports are stuck in LISTENING/unelected state. The tgm suite failures (clock class 248 = freerun, never 6) indicate the upstream Grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock; with the T-GM in freerun the entire downstream chain — BC → dualfollower slave — cannot lock, which is consistent with all suites failing in the same run. Additionally, the pmc bad command: GET / PARENT_DATA_SET errors in the dualnicbc suite (same run, linuxptp-daemon branch release-5.0) indicate a ptp4l management interface issue. The commit OCPBUGS-90708: Sync from upstream (22-Jun-2026) in linuxptp-daemon (merged 2026-06-23, two days after this run) and the commit OCPBUGS-88369: Fix CLOCK_REALTIME dual-publisher conflict in ParseTBCLogs in cloud-event-proxy (merged 2026-06-25) were both pending at the time of this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Likely pre-dates the fix in linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (OCPBUGS-90708 / CNF-24996, merged 2026-06-23, SHA 0459bc0) and cloud-event-proxy PR #701 (OCPBUGS-88369, merged 2026-06-25, SHA fc5d567) — both merged after this CI run on 2026-06-21. The breaking change introducing the regression is Unknown (upstream/downstream commit not visible in the shallow clone).
• Is it a flake? No — all four test suites in the same job (dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, tgm) failed with consistent, correlated errors. The T-GM staying at clock class 248 is a systematic fa


TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.

• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands
